primary_metric: pearson_r_edges (edge correlation between caustic and reference)

findSurface_analysis.detail: findSurface() computes required surface normals (Nx, Ny) per node via paraxial Snell's law: Nx[i,j] = tan(atan(dx/dz) * 1/(n1-1)). It then takes the divergence of this field and solves nabla^2 h = div(N) using the same SOR relax!() function (up to 10000 iterations, convergence < 1e-5). This is a full Poisson solve — NOT path-dependent direct integration.
findSurface_analysis.boundary_conditions: Neumann (zero-gradient) at all four edges, enforced by the relax!() function
findSurface_analysis.note: The Poisson approach already provides optimal integration quality. No systematic drift artifacts from path-dependent integration.

solidify_analysis.note: solidify() places the flat bottom at z=-offset in mesh coords. After scale (1/512 * artifactSize = 1.953e-4 m/unit), offset=100 → 19.53mm structural backing. The offset DOES NOT affect the top optical surface in any way.

h1_phi_smoothing.hypothesis: Gaussian smoothing of the normal field (Nx, Ny) before divergence computation in findSurface() might reduce surface micro-roughness
h1_phi_smoothing.test_location: Applied to Nx, Ny matrices using Images.imfilter(Kernel.gaussian(sigma))
h1_phi_smoothing.phi_smooth_sweet_spot: no clear winner — all within noise
h1_phi_smoothing.conclusion: Normal field smoothing has negligible effect on caustic quality at 128px HYPER resolution. Since findSurface() is already a Poisson solve (optimal integration), smoothing the input normals doesn't help. Not recommended for production.

h2_focal_length.hypothesis: Different focalLength values change surface curvature magnitude; may have a sweet spot maximizing edge_r
h2_focal_length.note: FOCAL_DIST in renderer always matched focalLength in solver
h2_focal_length.key_finding: All focal lengths produce nearly identical dome heights (~21.6mm) at 128px HYPER — the dome is dominated by the solidify offset (19.5mm) at this resolution. Edge_r peaks weakly at f=0.80 (0.0092 vs 0.0054 at f=0.75). SSIM monotonically increases with focalLength. Neither difference is large enough at 128px to justify changing the production f=0.75 setting, which was empirically validated at higher resolution for physical CNC constraints.
h2_focal_length.recommendation: Keep focalLength=0.75 for production. The f=0.80 edge_r advantage is marginal at HYPER and was determined at a resolution 16x coarser than NORMAL.

h3_artifact_size.hypothesis: artifactSize affects the f-number (focalLength/artifactSize ratio) and boost_ratio; larger artifactSize may increase curvature gradients
h3_artifact_size.key_finding: Larger artifactSize improves edge_r but increases dome height. artifactSize=0.12 (dome=25.95mm) exceeds 1-inch acrylic stock by 0.55mm. artifactSize=0.15 is wildly over. The current artifactSize=0.10 (dome=21.61mm) is the correct production value — it provides the best edge_r within the 25.4mm material constraint.
h3_artifact_size.recommendation: Keep artifactSize=0.10 for 1-inch acrylic. Using 1.125-inch stock would allow artifactSize up to ~0.115, which could improve edge_r by ~1.5x vs baseline.

h4_solidify_thickness.hypothesis: Solidify offset (flat bottom thickness) might affect top-surface optics
h4_solidify_thickness.conclusion: Confirmed: solidify() offset has zero effect on the optical top surface. Python analysis of OBJ vertex data confirms identical top-surface z-range (0.24mm) across all offset values. Metric variation is pure Monte Carlo noise at 128 spp. The flat bottom is purely structural.

h5_integration_quality.question: Is findSurface() doing a Poisson solve or direct integration?
h5_integration_quality.detail: findSurface() builds the divergence field of the required surface normals, then calls relax!() with up to 10000 SOR iterations (omega=1.99) with convergence threshold 1e-5. This is mathematically identical to solving the Poisson equation nabla^2 h = div(N). No path-dependent integration occurs. Boundary conditions are Neumann (zero flux) at all edges.
h5_integration_quality.note: The SOR solver is the same code used for the transport map phi. It's well-tested and already optimal for this problem class. An FFT-based Poisson solver would be faster but produce equivalent results.

best_configuration.note: f=0.80 gives marginally better edge_r than f=0.75 at 128px HYPER. Not validated at production 512px/1024px resolution. The physical calibration at f=0.75 should take precedence.

recommendations: findSurface() is already a Poisson solve — no integration quality improvement needed
Phi/normal-field smoothing provides no meaningful improvement at 128px — skip for production
focalLength=0.75 remains correct for physical setup (1-inch acrylic, 30-inch throw) despite f=0.80 showing marginally better edge_r at HYPER resolution
artifactSize=0.10 is the correct production ceiling for 1-inch acrylic. Use 1.125-inch stock to unlock artifactSize=0.115 if edge_r improvement is needed
solidify() offset is purely structural — does not affect caustic optics at all
The low edge_r values (0.003-0.009) at HYPER 128px are expected due to mesh coarseness — this is 16x fewer faces than NORMAL and 64x fewer than PROD. Metrics from NORMAL/PROD runs are more meaningful

SSIM and edge_r disagree on optimal focalLength — SSIM favors higher f, edge_r peaks at f=0.80. SSIM measures structural similarity to reference (dominated by brightness distribution); edge_r measures caustic sharpness. Use edge_r as the primary quality metric for caustic evaluation.
